texts.full_text: the deficiency of β-hexosaminidase A. This accumulation leads to neuronal dysfunction and eventual cell death. The exact mechanisms by which GM2 ganglioside accumulation causes neuronal toxicity are not fully understood, but several hypotheses have been proposed. These include the disruption of intracellular trafficking, impairment of autophagy, and induction of inflammatory responses \[[@R9],[@R10]\]. 3. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS {#S3} ======================== The clinical manifestations of GM2-gangliosidoses vary depending on the specific disease and the age of onset. Infantile-onset Tay-Sachs and Sandhoff diseases are the most severe forms, characterized by rapid neurodegeneration, seizures, and early death, typically within the first few years of life. Juvenile and adult-onset forms of the diseases have a more variable and slower progression, with symptoms including cognitive decline, psychiatric disturbances, and motor dysfunction. The B1 variant of GM2-gangliosidosis, which results from a specific mutation in the HEXA gene, presents with a milder and more slowly progressing form of the disease \[[@R11],[@R12]\]. 4. DIAGNOSIS {#S4} ============ The diagnosis of GM2-gangliosidoses typically involves a combination of clinical evaluation, biochemical testing, and genetic analysis. Biochemical testing includes measuring β-hexosaminidase A activity in blood or tissue samples. Genetic testing can identify specific mutations in the HEXA or HEXB genes. Early and accurate diagnosis is crucial for genetic counseling and for initiating any available therapeutic interventions as early as possible. 5. THERAPEUTIC STRATEGIES {#S5} ======================== Current therapeutic strategies for GM2-gangliosidoses are limited and primarily focus on symptom management and palliative care. However, several experimental approaches are being explored, including: - Enzyme Replacement Therapy (ERT): Although ERT has shown promise in other lysosomal storage disorders, its effectiveness in GM2-gangliosidoses is limited due to the inability of the enzyme to cross the blood-brain barrier. - Gene Therapy: As mentioned, gene therapy using viral vectors to deliver functional copies of the HEXA or HEXB genes is a promising approach. texts.full_text: is quite enamored with progress. It also shows other issues important to the more progressive elements of Edwardian society. It briefly discusses the rights of women. It takes a fairly critical view of religious authorities. It highlights the aristocratic tendencies that still survived. It also dealt with the issues of the deformed and disabled. Some of these concerns relate most to the author himself. He was the son of a minister who became a staunch agnostic. Judging by this book, his agnosticism went further than thinking some questions were unanswerable. Instead he seemed to believe that answers to such questions ultimately did not satisfy. The striving to find answers was what mattered. This makes the skepticism and mysticism of the work go together. texts.full_text: ngor Old Boys Football Club, also known as 1st Bangor O.B., is a Northern Irish, intermediate football club playing in the Premier Division of the Northern Amateur Football League. A club of the same name was in existence from 1938–1965, but the present club was founded in 1967, and joined the Amateur League in 1979, winning the Junior Cup in the same season. Originally from Bangor, County Down, the club is now based atBallyholme Road Sports Complex. The complex includes two full-sized pitches with floodlighting for evening games as well as changing facilities and a social bar area where members can gather after matches or training sessions to enjoy refreshments. 1st Bangor Old Boys F.C., which is also known by its nickname "The O.B.", has built up an impressive history since it was re-established in 1967. The club's home colors are black, red and white stripes with the away kit being blue shirts paired with yellow shorts—a distinctive color combination that makes them easily recognizable on any football field. Over time they have developed a strong community presence within Bangor County Down area as well as having an active youth academy system to nurture young talent. The club's commitment towards grassroots development has seen many of their players progress into senior teams, both locally and internationally over the years—an indication that 1st Old Boys F.C., are doing something right in terms of player training. The team competes at a high level within Northern Ireland’s amateur football league system. They have had numerous successful seasons including multiple promotions from lower divisions to their current Premier Division status

Under det kommande året kommer ett preliminärt utkast, men någon slutlig text kommer inte före april 2009. texts.full_text: ents with closed head injury. This brief review summarizes the current understanding of mild closed head injury with postconcussion syndrome. It is to be emphasiNeurodiagnostic testing in patients with closed head injury. This brief review summarizes the current understanding of mild closed head injury with postconcussion syndrome. It is to be emphasized that the diagnosis and management of these patients require a multidisciplinary approach. Neurodiagnostic testing plays a crucial role in the evaluation and management of patients with closed head injury. Common neurodiagnostic tests include: 1. Computed Tomography (CT) Scan: This is often the first-line imaging test used in the emergency setting to detect acute intracranial hemorrhage, skull fractures, and other structural abnormalities. 2. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI): This test provides more detailed images of the brain and is useful for detecting subtle abnormalities, such as diffuse axonal injury, which may not be visible on a CT scan. 3. Electroencephalography (EEG): This test measures the electrical activity of the brain and is useful for detecting seizures, which can be a complication of head injury. 4. Neuropsychological Testing: This involves a series of tests to assess cognitive function, including memory, attention, and executive function. It is particularly useful for evaluating the cognitive deficits associated with postconcussion syndrome. 5. texts.full_text: tted September 13, 1978. Decided November 29, 1978. *86 Gerald R. Pullen, Portland, argued the cause and filed a brief for appellant. Robert D. Puckett, of Proctor & Puckett, Klamath Falls, argued the cause and filed a brief for respondent. Before DENECKE, C.J.and LENT, JJ., submitted on reargument.* 87 The issue in this case is whether an order by which one co-personal representative of decedents' estate was removed as such without his consent and the other allowed to remain gave rise either to a cause or action for damages against both representatives. It appears that no Oregon cases have been found dealing with removal, under any circumstances, except where there has already existed an order authorizing it. 88 The question is whether one co-personal representative can be removed without his consent by the other and still retain their respective rights of recovery in a suit brought to enforce such right. In our opinion he cannot do so because both are equally entitled to bring action for damages under ORS 127, which provides that all persons who have an interest as creditors or legatees may sue jointly. 89 The facts here show no basis upon the record before us where either co-personal representative had any right of recovery against each other. A portion of the silicon nitride film on an area in which the second field oxide film is to be formed is removed by etching and, then, by using a LOCOS process again, the second field oxide film connected to the first film oxide film is formed on the N type epitaxial layer. In the junction between the first and second field oxide films, there is no step or recess formed. Therefore, it is possible to obtain a substantially complete flatness in the junction area. In the conventional element isolation region including the LOCOS type field oxide film and the U-trench isolation region which is buried with the polysilicon film, the flatness of the surface of element isolation region including the junction area of these two isolation reGions is maintained. However, there is a problem in the U-trench isolation region which is buried with the polysilicon film, regardless of the the structure and formation of the field insulating film. That is, the problem resides in the formation of the silicon oxide film on the upper end of the U-trench by the thermal oxidation of the top face of the polysilicon film burying the U-trench. This is not required by RFC 2781, // which specifies the handling of surrogate pairs and odd numbers of bytes. RFC 2781 is the standard that outlines the rules and specifications for UTF-16 and UTF-16BE encodings. This standard states that surrogate pairs (used for encoding characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane) and the handling of odd numbers of bytes (which indicate the encoding is not well-formed) should not generate an error. Instead, the decoding process should attempt to decode to the best of its ability, often by substituting a replacement character (such as the Unicode replacement character, U+FFFD) to indicate an error or an unexpected character encoding. However, the decision to handle encoding errors by substituting a replacement character or by returning an error depends on the specific requirements of the application or the context in which the encoding or decoding is being performed. In some cases, returning an error can be a more appropriate way to handle unexpected or malformed input, especially in cases where the input is being received from an untrusted or uncontrolled source. In other cases, where the application can tolerate some degree of malformed input or where the input is from a trusted source, using a replacement character can be a more user-friendly way of handling unexpected input, as it allows the application to continue to process the input to the extent possible,

texts.full_text: [Chronic defibrination syndrome and thrombocytopenia in splenic hamartoma (splenoma)]. In a 41-year-old woman who had suffered from hypofibrinogenemia, thrombocytopenia, leukopenia and splenomegaly for several years a causative hamartoma of the spleen (splenoma) was removed surgically. The results of a preoperative therapeutic trial with heparin, the marked accumulation of 125I-activity in the splenoma following intravenous injection of radioiodinated fibrinogen, the immunohistochemical demonstration of extensive fibrinogen deposits in the splenoma, and normalization of fibrinogen levels and blood cell counts after splenectomy all support the diagnosis of chronic defibrination syndrome and thrombocytopenia secondary to the splenic hamartoma. The hamartoma appeared to act as a sink for fibrinogen and platelets, leading to the chronic consumption and sequestration of these elements. Post-splenectomy, the patient experienced a significant improvement in her hematological parameters, including normalization of fibrinogen levels and blood cell counts, which underscores the critical role of the splenic hamartoma in the pathogenesis of her condition. This case highlights the importance of considering splenic hamartoma as a rare but treatable cause of chronic hypofibrinogenemia

texts.full_text: der, and the mechanisms by which the bladder responds to pathologic injury, are largely unknown. The experiments outlined in this proposal will test the hypothesis that two recently-discovered members of the EGF family, amphiregulin and heparin-binding epidermal growth factor-like growth factor (HB-EGF), are physiologic regulators of inflammatory processes in the bladder. We have recently shown that normal human bladder epithelial cells cultured under serum-free conditions express heparin-binding EGF- like factors with the molecular properties of HB-EGF and amphiregulin. HB-EGF and amphiregulin are potent stromal and epithelial regulatory factors which induce biologic effects via activation of the EGF receptor. HB-EGF is expressed by a variety of inflammatory cells, including T lymphocytes, eosinophils and macrophages, and is an extremely potent smooth muscle cell mitogen. Expression of high levels of HB-EGF by normal epithelial cells is a novel finding, and suggests a role for bladder epithelial cells in inflammatory processes. We hypothesize that these HB- EGF and amphiregulin may participate in mechanisms of bladder epithelial and smooth muscle hyperplasia, and possibly immunoregulatory responses, associated with inflammatory conditions and would healing responses in the human bladder. The specific aims are: Aim 1. (a)(A): To define at both protein expression levels by Western blotting techniques as well as mRNA transcriptional level using RT-PCR methods amphiregulin synthesis or secretion into culture supernatants of normal and pathologic urothelial cell lines, in addition to epithelial cells isolated from bladder tissue samples. Aim 1 (b): To define the biological effects that exogenous HB EGF administration has on human smooth muscle as well a variety inflammatory conditions through utilizing specific experimental assays designed for this purpose such cellular proliferation or migration measurements; apoptosis detection techniques including caspase activity analysis alongside annexin V staining procedures with flow cytometry readings taken before/after treatment peri

texts.full_text: tion is a palaeontological formation located in Kazakhstan. It dates to the Oligocene period. It is named after Paraceratherium, also commonly known as Indricotherium, an extinct genus of gigantic hornled rhinoceros. The formation is known for the well-preserved fossils of Indricotherium, which is considered the largest known terrestrial mammal of the Oligocene epoch. The fossils found here, which include massive skulls, rhinoceros-like horns, and limb bones, suggest Indricotherium roamed the floodplain and swampy environments of the region. texts.full_text: The Columbus Ohio Home Inspection Authority (614) 459-5941 Home Inspections Commercial Inspections Services Mold Radon Infrared Stucco Service Areas FHA 203K Staff Blog FAQ Fees Contact Radon Inspection Get your home radon inspection in Columbus, Ohio Exposure to Radon Causes Lung Cancer The deaths of Peter Jennings and Dana Reeve have raised public awareness about lung cancer. Lung cancer kills thousands of Americans every year. Smoking, radon, and second-hand smoke are the leading causes of lung cancer. Radon can cause lung cancer in people who have never smoked. Claudia Henschke, author of Lung Cancer: Myths, Facts-and Hope says that Radon which is found in soil and can seep into basements is a leading cause of lung cancer in nonsmokers. What is Radon? Radon is a colorless, odorless, tasteless, and chemically inert radioactive gas. It is formed by the natural radioactive decay of uranium in rock, soil, and water. Naturally existing, low levels of uranium occur widely in Earth's crust. It can be found in all 50 states. Unless you test for it, there is no way of telling how much is present. U.S. SurgeU.S. Surgeon General's Advisory The U.S. Surgeon General has issued an advisory regarding the dangers of radon exposure. The advisory highlights the importance of radon testing and mitigation to protect public health. According to the Surgeon General, radon is the leading cause of lung cancer among nonsmokers and is the second leading cause overall. The advisory encourages homeowners, renters, and building professionals to take action by testing for radon and fixing any issues that may arise. By doing so, it is possible to reduce the risk of lung cancer and save lives. The Columbus Ohio Home Inspection Authority is committed to providing comprehensive radon testing and mitigation services to ensure the safety and well-being of the community. For more information on radon and the Surgeon General's advisory, visit the official website or contact the Columbus Ohio Home Inspection Authority. Don't wait to take action – protect yourself and your loved ones from the risks of radon

texts.full_text: 2.. Material and method The evolutionary history of odobenids, or sea lions as they are commonly known today, is deeply intertwined with the North Pacific region. These fascinating marine mammals first appeared during the early Miocene epoch (approximately 23 to 16 million years ago) in this area \[[@RSOS180423C1], [@RSOS180423c2]\]. Their fossil record provides invaluable insights into their ancient diversity and how they evolved over time, reflecting changes not only within the odobenid group but also influenced by broader ecological dynamics of that era. In those early days on Earth's timeline when our modern oceans were taking shape alongside evolving marine ecosystems, these creatures shared habitats with other notable groups such as otariids (true seals) and desmatophocidae. Desmatophoca was a genus characterized not only by their larger body sizes but also likely occupying higher trophic levels in the food chain \[[@RSOS180423C1]--[@RSOS180423C6]\]. However, this dynamic changed with an extinction event that befell desmatofoacids during mid to late Miocene times. The demise of these larger species allowed for a diversification and increase in the size range within odobenid populations. This period saw not only increased diversity among sea lions but also markedly higher average body sizes, culminating with some truly massive individuals by latest MiocENE-early Pliocene times \[[@RSOS180423C3], [@RSOS180423C4]\]. These communities were often composed of otariids and two or more species from the large-bodied odobenid group, with body lengths reaching up to 2.5 meters. In a surprising turn that defies this trend towards gigantism in their lineage is our newly discovered mid-late Miocene sea lion hailing from Baja California Sur deposits of Mexico \[[@RSOS180423C7]\]. This diminutive odobenid stands out for its relatively small size, presenting an instance where dwarfing occurred within a group that had been

texts.full_text: More than $500,000 raised over the years for son's group home On Aug. 3, Michael Shanahan jumped on his bike at 5:15 a.m. and began pedaling. Over the next two days, the River Forest resident covered 500 miles—nearly 125 miles daily—through Wisconsin’s rolling hills and bustling cities, all to support PACTT Vocational Services, the organization providing care for his son, Marty, and others with developmental disabilities. The journey, which began in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, tested Shanahan’s endurance. Rain delayed his first day’s progress, but volunteers with signs reading “Pedal for PACTT” and local businesses offering rest stops kept him motivated. “Every mile was better than the last, but knowing Marty and his friends depend on this? It’s nothing,” Shanahan said during a mid-ride interview. Along the route, donors contributed via a dedicated fundraising page, swelling the total to over $75

texts.full_text: and Castlemartyr village. He also managed the County Cork estates of his second wife's father, Lord Burlington, and proved himself an excellent steward of the family property. In 1707, he was elected to the Irish House of Commons for Midleton, a seat he held until 1713, and then sat for Kilmallock from 1713 to 1715. Between 1715 and 1756 he represented County Cork. He quickly gained a prominent role in Parliament and Sir Robert Walpole is said to have styled him "the King of the IrishHouse". Boyle was appointed as Chancellor of the Exchequer for Ireland from 24 June 1730 until his death, making him one of the longest-serving holders of that office. He also served in other important positions such as Lord Justice (acting head) during King George II's absence between January and May 1756. Boyle was created Baron Boyle on 20 June 1734, Viscount Shannon on 9 July of the same year, and Earl of Shannon in December 1756 by patent with remainder to his son-in-law Henry Ponsonby (later Lord Duncannon). He was also made a Privy Councillor for Ireland. In addition to his political career, Boyle had an interest in architecture and gardening. Under his direction, the gardens at Castlemartyr were greatly improved with new plantings of trees and shrubs as well as ornamental features such as statues and fountains. He also commissioned a number of buildings on the estate including stables designed by Davis Ducarte in 1730-45, which are considered among Ireland's finest examples from that period. Boyle married twice: firstly to Jane Ponsonby (died c. 28 December 1693), daughter of William Ponsoby and Lady Mary Moore; secondly on 7 March 1705/6, he wed Catherine Coote (c. 14 February 1685 – April 1763) who was the only child from her father's first marriage to Elizabeth Burlington née Villiers daughter of Edward Hyde Lord Clarendon and Frances Howard Countess Suffolk & Berkshire by whom he had several children including: - Henry Boyle, Viscount Shannon (1708–24 February 1763) w

texts.full_text: In Israel, Luxembourg Industries markets about 60 registered products through agricultural input distributors who handle direct sales to the growers. They keep in very close contact with the growers in the Israeli market through a network of skilled company field representatives. These representatives accompany the growers, manage end-row meetings, field trials, discuss crop protection issues and devise solutions. Internationally Luxembourg Industries markets their products through their subsidiary offices in the USA, Brazil, Serbia and Argentina and represented in other countries by local companies. Information about the products and activities in each country, is available through listed subsidiary’s website. Advanced Production Facilities Luxembourg’s production and formulation facilities operate in a carefully controlled, computerized setting to assure high quality, high yield and to protect its workers and the environment. The products are synthesized and formulated on the basis of in-house developed processes and know-how agreements with leading international producers. Research, Development & Registration Product activity studies are performed in the field by agronomists, biologists, crop protection specialists and growers, and are carried out in cooperation with academic institutions. The data generated from field trials supports product registrations in the countries where we are marketing our products and provides the base for the instructions printed on our product labels. Luxembourg Industries has it own in-house Chemistry R&D team that works at laboratories and a pilot facility located at the production sites. They maintain close professional connections with academic institutions and with the distinguished research community. Luxembourg’s registration team works to maintain and expand registrations of its products in the countries where it is active and works closely with the registration authorities. Israel Luxembourg Industries Ltd main@luxembourg.co.il www.luxembourg.co.il Brazil Luxembourg do Brasil Ms. Milena Pessini milena@luxembourg.com.br

texts.full_text: ke you're experiencing a common issue where `Type.GetType` fails to retrieve an existing type from an already loaded assembly due to differences in case sensitivity or culture-specific information. Here are some steps and suggestions that might help resolve your problem: 1. Check the AssemblyQualifiedName: Ensure that the `AssemblyQualifiedName` you're using exactly matches the one of the loaded assembly, including casing and cultural aspects. For example: ``` string AQN = "MyNamespace.MyType, MyAssembly, Version=1.0.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=null"; Type type = Type.GetType(AQN); ``` 2. Use `GetExportedTypes`: Instead of using `Type.GetType`, try getting the types from the assembly directly: ```csharp Assembly myAssembly = ...; // Load your assembly here if not already loaded. Type[] types = myAssembly.GetExportedTypes(); foreach (var type in types) { Console.WriteLine(type.FullName); } ``` 3. Check for binding redirects: If you're working with different versions of the same assembly, make sure there are appropriate `<bindingRedirect>` entries in your `app.config` or `web.config`: ```xml <runtime> <assemblyBinding xmlns="urn:schemas-microsoft-com:asm.v1"> <dependentAssembly> <assemblyIdentity name="MyAssembly" publicKeyToken="..." culture="neutral"/> <bindingRedirect oldVersion="0.0.0.0-9999999999" newVersion="x.x.x.x"/> </dependentAssembly> </assemblyBinding> </runtime> ``` 4. texts.full_text: Home A - Z Directory Contact LOCUS Loyola University Chicago University Newsroom BACK TO NEWSROOM BACK TO PRESS RELEASES Media Contacts Anna Rozenich Director, University Communications arozenich@luc.edu 312.915.6164 News Announcements Archive for April, 2008 Loyola Holds 2008 Women’s Leadership Conference School of Law Appoints Chair in Constitutional Law Loyola University Chicago Department of Computer Science Nets $600,000 Grant from the National Science Foundation Press Release For Immediate Distribution: April [Date], 2008 Contact: Anna Rozenich Director, University Communications arozenich@luc.edu 312.915-6164 Loyola’s Department of Computer Science Secures $600K Grant from the National Sciences Foundation CHICAGO—The Loyola University Chicago (LUChicago) College of Arts and sciences’ department has received a significant grant worth half-a-million dollars ($379,528 for year 1; total: approximately$4.5 million over five years), awarded by The U.S National Science Foundation to support research in computer science education. The funding will be used primarily towards enhancing the university’s curriculum and supporting faculty-led initiatives aimed at advancing computational thinking among students across various disciplines within LUChicago, including but not limited to biologyinformatics (the intersection of biological sciences with information technology). This grant is a testament both Loyola's commitment toward fostering innovation in computer science education as well its recognition by national bodies such the NSF for excellence and leadership. “We are thrilled about this opportunity,” said Dr. [Name], Chairperson, Department Of Computer Science at LUChicago.“The support from The National Sciences Foundation will enable us to develop cutting-edge educational programs that prepare our students not only with technical skills but also critical thinking abilities necessary in today’s rapidly evolving technological landscape.” This grant is expected significantly impact the department's ability attract and retain top-tier faculty members while providing unparalleled learning experiences for undergraduate, graduate as well professional development opportunities. For more information about Loyola University Chicago or this particular initiative within its Department Of Computer Science please contact Anna Rozenich at arozensch@luc.edu.

texts.full_text: to get She missed their meaning. She didn’t realize her parents were now preparing for a shotgun wedding. That mid-February day came, bedecked with roses and white calla lilies throughout the church. Gordon in his best brown suit. Mary in a simple lace eggshell dress and netted veil. Family and friends with a lot of questions. The pastor happily married the couple. He said that they had fit in more time getting to know one another, as well as chat with the pastor, than most couples fit into six months! One of Mary’s fellow teachers at the school was so enthralled with this whirlwind love affair, that she began dating one of Gordon’s co-workers from the construction site, too. They, too, were quickly married. That did not end well. 40 years of ups and downs; trials and tribulations – Gordon and Mary still loved each other. They both still thought of one another as the answer to their prayers. Raising children and enjoying grandchildren. Gordon died of lung cancer in late 2006. Mary didn’t last long without him. She died in early 2008 of colon cancer and a broken heart. They’re together again now. This is a true story. The best ones often are. If only we could all find a love like Gordon and Mary. My parents. Stepping into the sacred space was like a portal back in time. No one specific time. More of a plane open and safe for all ages to comingle at once. The mist in the air grew thicker as I stepped through this old graveyard. The grass wet around my tenni-shoes. Searching for old Colonial names linked to my own family. As the search continued, my clothes became more soaked with moisture. The weather progressed to a drizzle of rain. Nothing on my person was safe from the wet. Even my camera clasped in my hand inside my jacket pocket had small droplets smeared away as much
